turns.content: I've read about the tauri crate and how it uses Rust for the backend, but I still don't fully understand how exactly the JavaScript frontend talks to that Rust backend without writing a single line of native code.

The magic happens through the IPC mechanism which acts as a bridge between your tauri::script module and the Rust side. When you call a function in JavaScript, it triggers an event that gets routed to a specific handler inside your Rust code, allowing you to define what happens when that command is executed without ever touching C or assembly.
That sounds convenient, but isn't this just a fancy way of doing manual memory management with raw pointers? I feel like we're still dealing with the same complexity under the hood.

Not at all. The tauri crate actually wraps your Rust logic in safe abstractions that handle serialization and deserialization automatically so you never deal with raw pointers or manual memory management. You just define functions on the impl trait for your command handlers, and the framework takes care of sending data back and forth safely.
